items.q: What does the teleological (design) argument claim?
items.correct: Apparent purpose, order or complexity in the universe implies an intelligent designer — Paley's watchmaker analogy is the classic example.
items.misconception: The design argument proves God created the world in seven days.
items.random_wrong: The design argument proves evolution is false.
items.why: The argument is philosophical, not anti-evolutionary. Many religious thinkers accept evolution as the designer's mechanism — students conflate design with young-earth creationism.

items.q: What is the doctrine of the Trinity?
items.correct: God is one being existing in three persons (Father, Son, Holy Spirit) — distinct in person but united in essence; Christians remain monotheistic.
items.misconception: Christians believe in three different gods.
items.random_wrong: The Trinity refers to heaven, earth and hell.
items.why: Students treat 'three persons' as 'three gods'. The orthodox position is one God in three distinct persons sharing one substance — a notoriously difficult concept students often misrepresent as polytheism.

items.correct: It is more accurately described as henotheistic or monistic — many gods (devas) are considered aspects or manifestations of one ultimate reality (Brahman).
items.misconception: Hinduism is straightforwardly polytheistic with millions of separate gods.
items.random_wrong: Hinduism worships no gods, only ancestors.
items.why: The 'many gods' surface obscures the underlying unity in much Hindu theology. Many traditions hold all deities to be expressions of one Brahman — students miss the monistic underpinning.

items.q: Whose 1802 Natural Theology uses the watchmaker analogy?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: William Paley. “David Hume” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Swinburne's defence of theism is best described as which?
items.why: Correct answer: A cumulative Bayesian case. “A modal ontological argument” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was Paley's Natural Theology published?
items.why: Exact answer: 1802. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
